constraint_note: No jealousy words. Show it through a character's actions, attention, or body language.
example: 'She scrolled past the photo three times, then went back to it. Zoomed in on the background.' — The behavior IS the jealousy.

description: Write a 4-line poem about tea or coffee. Line 1: the ritual of making it. Line 2: the first sip. Line 3: what you think about while drinking it. Line 4: the empty cup.
action: Write a 4-line poem about tea or coffee.
context: Small rituals are rich material. A cup of tea contains a whole world — the making, the tasting, the thinking, the ending. This trains you to find depth in the everyday.
constraint_note: Line 1: the ritual of making it. Line 2: the first sip. Line 3: what you think about while drinking. Line 4: the empty cup.
example: 'The kettle screams and I pour slow. / The first sip burns the tip of my tongue. / I think about the email I haven't sent. / The cup cools before I finish.' — Each line is a micro-moment.

description: Write a scene where a character cleans out a dead relative's closet. 150 words. Each item they touch should trigger a flash of memory — but keep each memory to one sentence. Accumulation creates emotion.
action: Write a scene of a character cleaning out a dead relative's closet. 150 words.
context: Objects left behind carry the weight of a person's life. Each item touched is a memory surfaced. This is one of the most emotionally rich scenarios in personal writing.
constraint_note: Each item they touch should trigger a brief memory or feeling. 150 words.
example: 'A shirt she'd never seen him wear. A receipt from a restaurant she didn't recognize.' — Unknown objects are more haunting than familiar ones.

description: Write 10 verbs that describe how different people walk: a soldier, a toddler, a bride, a thief, a drunk, a dancer, a doctor in a hurry, an old man, a teenager, a monk. One verb each. Precision in verbs is the fastest way to improve your prose.
action: Write 10 verbs that describe how different people walk.
context: Movement verbs are character shortcuts. 'Strode' and 'shuffled' create two completely different people in one word. This builds your verb vocabulary.
constraint_note: A soldier, a toddler, a bride, a thief, a drunk, a dancer, a doctor, a student, an old man, a teenager. One verb each.
example: 'The soldier marched. The toddler tottered. The thief slipped.' — One verb per person, and they're alive.

description: Write a scene where two strangers share an umbrella in the rain. 150 words. No romance — just the awkwardness and small kindness of proximity between people who don't know each other.
action: Write a scene of two strangers sharing an umbrella in the rain. 150 words.
context: Forced proximity between strangers creates natural tension. The umbrella is a tiny shared space — the awkwardness of bodies too close, the negotiation of pace.
constraint_note: No romance. Just the awkwardness and small kindness of sharing space with a stranger. 150 words.
example: 'He tilted it toward her without asking. She walked faster to match his stride. Neither spoke.' — Kindness without words.

description: Write about a sound that makes you feel safe. Not why it makes you feel safe — just the sound itself, described in enough detail that a reader could almost hear it. 60 words.
action: Write about a sound that makes you feel safe. 60 words.
context: Sound is the most emotionally direct sense — it bypasses analysis and goes straight to feeling. Writing about a safe sound trains you to access emotion through sensory specificity.
constraint_note: 60 words. Describe the sound itself in enough detail that a reader could almost hear it. Don't explain why it's safe — let the description do the work.
example: 'The low hum of the fridge at 2 AM, the house asleep around it.' — The reader feels the safety without being told.

description: Write a 100-word scene where a character is underwater. Describe the distortion: muffled sounds, blurred vision, pressure, the strange slowness. Unusual physical states force unusual language — that's the exercise.
action: Write a scene of a character underwater. 100 words.
context: Underwater distorts everything — sound is muffled, vision blurs, time slows. It's one of the most sensory-rich environments you can write.
constraint_note: Describe the distortion: muffled sounds, blurred vision, the pressure, the silence. 100 words.
example: 'Everything went blue and quiet. Her own heartbeat was the loudest sound.' — Underwater strips the world to essentials.

description: Write a scene where a character gives a gift. The gift is wrong — not what the other person wanted. 100 words. The wrong gift reveals more about a relationship than the right one.
action: Write a scene where a character gives a wrong gift. 100 words.
context: The wrong gift reveals a gap between how someone sees you and who you actually are. It's a small betrayal of understanding — and rich dramatic material.
constraint_note: The gift is wrong — not what the other person wanted. Show the moment of receiving. 100 words.
example: 'She unwrapped it slowly. A cookbook. She hadn't cooked in three years. She said thank you.' — The wrong gift says more than the right one.

description: Write a 150-word scene that takes place entirely in a moving car. Two people. One is driving, one is a passenger. The power dynamic of driver vs. passenger shapes every conversation. Use it.
action: Write a scene in a moving car. Two people — driver and passenger. 150 words.
context: Cars create forced intimacy. The driver has power (controls the space), the passenger is captive. Eye contact is optional. This shapes every conversation that happens inside.
constraint_note: Use the driver/passenger dynamic. 150 words.
example: 'He kept his eyes on the road. She watched the buildings pass. Neither reached for the radio.' — The car is a pressure cooker.

description: Write about the last dream you remember. Not interpreted — just described. Dreams have their own logic: impossible transitions, emotional non-sequiturs, familiar places that look wrong. Capture that strangeness. 100 words.
action: Write about the last dream you remember. Not interpreted — just described.
context: Dreams have their own logic: impossible transitions, familiar strangers, emotions without cause. Describing a dream without interpreting it trains you to write surreal scenes.
constraint_note: Just describe what happened. Don't interpret or analyze. Let the dream logic stand on its own. 100 words.
example: 'I was in my school but it was also a hospital. My mother was there but she was younger than me.' — Dream logic needs no explanation.

description: Write a scene where a character is eating alone in a restaurant. 100 words. Make the reader feel either the peace of solitude or the sting of loneliness — your choice — without ever naming either.
action: Write a scene of a character eating alone in a restaurant. 100 words.
context: Eating alone can be peaceful or lonely — same action, different framing. Your job is to make the reader feel one or the other without stating it.
constraint_note: Make the reader feel either the peace of solitude or the ache of loneliness. Don't state which. 100 words.
example: Peace: 'She ordered without looking at the menu and opened her book.' Loneliness: 'She ordered without looking at the menu and checked her phone.'

description: Write 5 different ways to describe a character crying. No tears, no sobbing — find 5 other physical manifestations. A jaw tightening. Breathing through the mouth. Blinking too fast. Push past the cliché.
action: Write 5 different ways to describe a character crying — without tears or sobbing.
context: Crying is one of the most over-described emotions in fiction. Finding other physical manifestations — jaw tightening, breathing changing, hands gripping — makes it feel real instead of performed.
constraint_note: No tears, no sobbing. Find 5 other physical manifestations of crying.
example: 'Her jaw locked. She pressed her thumbnail into her palm until it left a mark.' — The body finds other ways to express what the eyes won't.

description: Write a 100-word scene set in a queue. Any queue — grocery store, temple, ticket counter. Capture the specific boredom and micro-dramas of waiting in line with strangers.
action: Write a scene set in a queue. 100 words.
context: Queues are micro-societies. The specific boredom, the small observations, the unspoken rules — there's a whole world in waiting.
constraint_note: Any queue — grocery, temple, ticket counter. Capture the specific boredom and the small human details. 100 words.
example: 'The man ahead counted his coins twice. The woman behind sighed at exactly the right volume to be heard.' — Queues reveal people.

description: Write a scene where a character lies to protect someone. 150 words. The lie should be small but the stakes should feel large. Good fiction finds the weight in small moments.
action: Write a scene where a character lies to protect someone. 150 words.
context: Protective lies are morally complex — the liar is doing something wrong for a right reason. This ambiguity is the engine of compelling fiction.
constraint_note: The lie should be small but the stakes should feel large. 150 words.
example: 'It wasn't that bad,' she said, putting the report card in her bag before he could see it. — Small lie, big love.

description: Write a paragraph that is one single sentence. 80+ words, grammatically correct, flowing from clause to clause. This builds your ability to sustain rhythm and control complex syntax. Read it aloud to check the flow.
action: Write a paragraph that is one single sentence. 80+ words, grammatically correct.
context: The long sentence builds a wave — clause flowing into clause, carrying the reader forward without a place to stop. It's a rhythm tool for building momentum or breathlessness.
constraint_note: One sentence, 80+ words, grammatically correct. Flow from clause to clause.
example: Think of it as a river — each clause feeds into the next, and the reader can't stop until the period arrives.

description: Write a scene set at a wedding where one guest is deeply unhappy. 150 words. Everyone else is celebrating. Show the contrast through what this character notices that no one else does.
action: Write a wedding scene where one guest is deeply unhappy. 150 words.
context: Contrast is one of fiction's most powerful tools. Joy surrounding sadness — or sadness inside joy — amplifies both. The celebration makes the unhappiness sharper.
constraint_note: Everyone else is celebrating. Show the contrast between the event and the one person's inner state. 150 words.
example: 'She clapped when everyone clapped. She smiled when the camera turned.' — Performing happiness at a celebration is its own kind of loneliness.

description: Write 6 sentences about a city you've visited. Each sentence must use a different verb of movement: 'The traffic crawled. The pigeons scattered. The crowd surged.' Verbs of movement bring places alive.
action: Write 6 sentences about a city you've visited. Each sentence must use a different verb of movement.
context: Verbs of movement bring places alive. 'The traffic crawled' is more vivid than 'there was traffic.' This trains you to reach for specific, kinetic verbs instead of static description.
constraint_note: 6 sentences, 6 different movement verbs. Think: crawled, scattered, surged, drifted, stumbled, rushed.
example: 'The autorickshaws weaved through gaps that didn't exist. The chai wallah's hand darted between glasses.' — Movement verbs make the reader feel present.

description: Write a 100-word scene where a character is hiding. Where are they? What can they hear? What are they afraid of? The reader should feel the confinement and the fear of being found.
action: Write a scene of a character hiding. 100 words.
context: Hiding activates all the senses — hearing sharpens, breathing becomes conscious, every sound is a threat. It's one of the most physically immersive scenarios you can write.
constraint_note: Where are they? What can they hear? What are they afraid of? 100 words.
example: 'She pressed her back against the wall and held her breath. Footsteps. Then nothing. Then footsteps again.' — Hiding is pure sensory writing.

description: Think of a teacher who affected you. Write 80 words about one specific moment with them — not a summary of their impact, but one scene. What did they say? Where were you standing?
action: Write about one specific moment with a teacher who affected you. 80 words.
context: One scene is more powerful than a summary. 'She changed my life' means nothing. 'She handed back my essay with one sentence circled and said, this is the real you' — that means everything.
constraint_note: Not a summary of their impact — one scene. What did they say? Where were you standing? What did the room look like?
example: 'He stopped mid-lecture, looked at me, and said: read that last line again, but slower. The room went quiet.' — One moment, fully rendered.

description: Write a 100-word scene where two characters are assembling furniture together. Through how they collaborate (or don't), reveal whether their relationship is strong or falling apart. No dialogue about the relationship itself.
action: Write a scene of two characters assembling furniture together. 100 words.
context: Collaborative tasks reveal relationships. How people work together — patient or impatient, leading or following — shows more than any conversation about the relationship.
constraint_note: Through how they collaborate (or don't), reveal whether the relationship is strong or falling apart. No dialogue about the relationship itself.
example: 'She held the shelf while he searched for the right screw. He handed her the wrong one. She didn't correct him.' — The small failures tell the story.

description: Write 10 sentences that each contain a color, but the color is doing unexpected work: 'a grey kind of Tuesday,' 'her voice went white,' 'the argument turned a deep orange.' Colors as emotional shorthand.
action: Write 10 sentences where a color does unexpected emotional work.
context: Colors as emotional shorthand — 'a grey kind of Tuesday,' 'her voice went white' — expand your descriptive palette beyond the literal.
constraint_note: Each sentence must use a color, but not to describe a visual. Use color as mood, sound, feeling, or atmosphere.
example: 'The argument turned a deep orange.' / 'It was a beige kind of sadness — not dramatic, just flat.' — Color becomes emotion.

description: Write a scene where a character is lost — physically lost in an unfamiliar place. 100 words. Focus on the sensory disorientation: nothing looks familiar, signs are unreadable, distances feel wrong.
action: Write a scene of a character physically lost in an unfamiliar place. 100 words.
context: Being lost activates every sense — you notice everything because nothing is familiar. The sensory hyperawareness of lostness is rich writing material.
constraint_note: Focus on the sensory experience of not knowing where you are. 100 words.
example: 'Every street looked the same. The signs were in a script she couldn't read. She turned left because left felt right.' — Lost is a sensory state.

description: Write a 150-word scene that ends with a single-word sentence. Build toward it. That final word should land like a punch. This teaches you the power of ending on impact.
action: Write a 150-word scene that ends with a single-word sentence.
context: A single-word ending lands like a punch — but only if you build toward it. The preceding sentences should create momentum that the final word stops dead.
constraint_note: Build toward the final word. It should feel both surprising and inevitable. 150 words.
example: 'She packed her bags. She called a cab. She looked at the apartment one last time. She left the key on the table. Gone.' — The single word carries the weight of everything before it.

description: Describe the oldest person you know. Not their age — their oldness. The way they move, pause, remember. The texture of their skin, the pace of their speech. 100 words of pure observation.
action: Describe the oldest person you know. Not their age — their oldness. 80 words.
context: Age is not a number — it's a way of moving, pausing, remembering. The texture of oldness is in the body, the pace, the hands.
constraint_note: The way they move, pause, remember. The texture of age. 80 words.
example: 'She lowered herself into the chair in stages, as if negotiating with her knees.' — Oldness is physical, not numerical.

description: Write a scene where a character is swimming. 100 words. Capture the physical experience: the resistance of water, the rhythm of breathing, the muffled world below the surface. Physical writing is underrated.
action: Write a scene of a character swimming. 100 words.
context: Swimming is one of the most physical experiences you can write — resistance, rhythm, breath, temperature. It forces you into the body.
constraint_note: Capture the physical experience: resistance of water, rhythm of strokes, breathing. 100 words.
example: 'She pushed off the wall and the water closed over her. For three strokes, no sound.' — Swimming is sensory immersion.

description: Write a 4-line poem where each line is a lie. The poem should still feel emotionally true. This is the paradox of fiction: invented facts can carry real feeling.
action: Write a 4-line poem where every line is a lie.
context: The paradox of fiction: invented facts can carry real feeling. A poem of lies that feels emotionally true reveals something about how truth works in writing — it's not about accuracy, it's about resonance.
constraint_note: Every line must be factually untrue. But the poem as a whole should feel emotionally honest.
example: 'I never think about you. / The house was always this quiet. / I chose this. / I'm fine.' — Every line is a lie, but the poem tells the truth.

description: Write about an act of kindness you witnessed between strangers. 80 words. Don't editorialize — just describe what happened. Let the reader feel it without being told how to feel.
action: Write about an act of kindness you witnessed between strangers. 80 words.
context: Observed kindness is powerful writing material because it's unperformed — people being good when they don't know they're being watched. This trains you to notice and record without editorializing.
constraint_note: Just describe what happened. No commentary, no 'it restored my faith in humanity.' Let the reader feel it without being told how to feel.
example: 'The man in front of me at the counter turned around and paid for the woman behind him. She started to protest. He shook his head and left.' — Show, don't tell.

description: Write a scene where a character is packing lunch for someone else. 100 words. Through what they pack and how they pack it, reveal how they feel about the person. No thoughts, no dialogue.
action: Write a scene of a character packing lunch for someone else. 100 words.
context: Care is best shown through small, specific actions. What someone packs and how they pack it reveals love, resentment, obligation, or indifference — without a word spoken.
constraint_note: Through what they pack and how they pack it, reveal how they feel about the person. No thoughts, no dialogue.
example: 'She cut the crusts off, then stopped. He was thirty-four. She left them on.' — One gesture, a whole relationship.

description: Write 5 opening lines that each start with a number. 'Three things I know about my sister.' 'It took eleven minutes.' 'There were forty-two steps to the roof.' Numbers create instant specificity and intrigue.
action: Write 5 opening lines that each start with a number.
context: Numbers in opening lines create instant specificity. They promise precision and make the reader trust the narrator — someone who counts is someone who pays attention.
constraint_note: Each line starts with a number. Each should feel like the opening of a different story.
example: 'Three things I know about my sister.' / 'It took eleven minutes.' / 'There were forty-two steps to the roof.' — Numbers ground fiction in reality.

description: Write a 150-word scene set in a library. But something is wrong — the library is too quiet, or a book is missing, or someone is watching. Build unease without anything overtly threatening happening.
action: Write a scene set in a library where something is wrong. 150 words.
context: Disrupting a familiar, safe space creates unease. A library that's too quiet, a missing book, a stranger — the wrongness is amplified by the setting's expected calm.
constraint_note: Something is off — too quiet, a book missing, someone who shouldn't be there. 150 words.
example: 'Every shelf was full except one. A single gap, eye-level, where something had been removed recently.' — Small wrongness in a calm space is more unsettling than chaos.

description: Write about the gap between who you are at home and who you are in public. Not an essay — a scene. Show both versions through action. 100 words.
action: Write about the gap between who you are at home and who you are in public. Show both versions through action. 100 words.
context: Everyone performs a version of themselves in public. The gap between the performed self and the private self is one of the richest territories in personal writing.
constraint_note: Not an essay — a scene. Show both versions through what you do, not what you think. 100 words.
example: 'In the meeting I nodded and said, great idea. At home I stood in the kitchen eating cold rice from the pot with my hands.' — The contrast does the work.

description: Write a scene where a character is bargaining at a street market. 100 words. The negotiation should reveal something about both the buyer and the seller beyond the transaction.
action: Write a scene of a character bargaining at a street market. 100 words.
context: Negotiation reveals character. How someone bargains — aggressive, apologetic, playful — tells you about their relationship with money, power, and other people.
constraint_note: The negotiation should reveal something about the character beyond the transaction. 100 words.
example: 'He named a price he knew was too low. The seller didn't blink. He raised it before she had to.' — The bargaining style IS the character.

description: Write 3 different descriptions of the moon. One romantic, one scientific, one menacing. Same moon — three completely different moods. This is tone control, the most underrated writing skill.
action: Write 3 different descriptions of the moon: romantic, scientific, and menacing.
context: Same object, three tones. This trains you to control register deliberately — the moon doesn't change, your lens does. Tone is a choice, not an accident.
constraint_note: Same moon, three completely different emotional registers. Each should feel like a different genre.
example: Romantic: 'The moon hung low, close enough to touch.' Scientific: 'A waning gibbous, 94% illuminated.' Menacing: 'The moon watched from behind the clouds.'

description: Write a 100-word scene where a character is watching someone sleep. Not creepy — tender. A parent watching a child, a lover watching a partner. What do people look like when they're unguarded?
action: Write a scene of a character watching someone sleep. Tender, not creepy. 100 words.
context: Watching someone sleep is an act of unguarded attention. A parent watching a child, a lover watching a partner — the vulnerability of the sleeper and the tenderness of the watcher.
constraint_note: A parent and child, or a couple. Tender. 100 words.
example: 'She counted his breaths without meaning to. His hand was curled around the edge of the blanket.' — Tenderness is in the small observations.

description: Write a scene where a character accidentally breaks something that belongs to someone else. 100 words. The object matters less than the moment of breakage and the decision that follows: confess or hide it?
action: Write a scene where a character accidentally breaks something that belongs to someone else. 100 words.
context: The moment of breaking — and the instant after — reveals character. Do they confess? Hide it? The object matters because it belongs to someone else.
constraint_note: The object matters. The reaction matters more. 100 words.
example: 'The handle came off in her hand. She stood there holding it, listening for footsteps.' — The pause after breaking is the story.

description: Write a 150-word scene set at a funeral. But focus on one small, inappropriate detail: someone's phone ringing, a child laughing, an ugly tie. The small wrong thing at a funeral says more than grief described directly.
action: Write a funeral scene focused on one small, inappropriate detail. 150 words.
context: The small wrong thing at a funeral — a phone ringing, a child laughing — says more than grief described directly. Disruption reveals what formality tries to contain.
constraint_note: Focus on the inappropriate detail, not the grief. A phone ringing, an ugly tie, someone laughing. 150 words.
example: 'Halfway through the eulogy, a phone rang. Everyone knew whose it was. No one looked.' — The disruption IS the emotion.

description: Write 5 sentences where each one zooms in closer, like a camera. Start wide (the city), then the street, then the building, then the room, then a hand holding a pen. This is cinematic writing.
action: Write 5 sentences that zoom in like a camera — from wide shot to extreme close-up.
context: Cinematic writing controls the reader's attention by narrowing focus. Wide → close is one of the most effective ways to draw a reader into a scene.
constraint_note: Start wide (city), then street, then building, then room, then a specific detail (a hand holding a pen). Each sentence tightens the frame.
example: 'The city hummed under grey clouds. On Residency Road, a bus lurched to a stop. Behind a second-floor window, a desk lamp flickered. A notebook lay open. A hand paused mid-sentence.' — Each sentence zooms in.

description: Write a scene where a character is dancing alone in their room. 100 words. What song? How do they move? What do they look like when no one is watching? Solitary joy is hard to write well.
action: Write a scene of a character dancing alone in their room. 100 words.
context: Dancing alone is one of the most private, joyful, unperformed things a person can do. It's a character at their most authentic.
constraint_note: What song? How do they move? What do they look like when no one's watching? 100 words.
example: 'She turned the volume up and closed her eyes. Her movements were nothing like dancing and entirely like dancing.' — Private joy is hard to write and worth the effort.

description: Write about a word you love. Not its meaning — its sound, shape, the way your mouth moves when you say it. 60 words. Writers should have a physical relationship with language.
action: Write about a word you love — not its meaning, but its sound and shape. 60 words.
context: Writers should have a physical relationship with language. Words are not just meaning-carriers — they have texture, rhythm, and mouth-feel. This trains you to notice the material quality of language.
constraint_note: 60 words. Focus on: how your mouth moves when you say it, what it sounds like, what it looks like on the page.
example: 'Murmur. The lips close twice. It sounds like what it means — a low, private sound that doesn't want to be heard.' — The word becomes a sensory experience.

description: Write a 200-word scene where nothing happens. A character sits on a bench. People pass. The light changes. Make the reader want to keep reading anyway. If you can make stillness compelling, you can write anything.
action: Write a 200-word scene where nothing happens. A character sits on a bench. People pass. The light changes.
context: Making nothing feel like something is one of the hardest skills in writing. If the reader keeps reading when nothing is happening, your prose has earned their attention.
constraint_note: No events, no conflict, no dialogue. Just a person, a bench, and time passing. 200 words. Make the reader stay.
example: 'A pigeon landed near his foot, considered him, and left.' — When nothing happens, every small detail becomes significant.

description: Write a scene where a character receives a compliment they don't believe. 100 words. How do they deflect? What does their body do? Discomfort with praise reveals deep character.
action: Write a scene where a character receives a compliment they don't believe. 100 words.
context: Discomfort with praise reveals deep character. How someone deflects — humor, dismissal, subject change — tells you what they believe about themselves.
constraint_note: Show the deflection. What does their body do? How do they redirect? 100 words.
example: 'You're really talented.' She laughed and looked at her hands. 'I just got lucky with the prompt.' — The deflection is the character.

description: Write 8 lines that each start with 'Before I knew...' Each line should reveal something the speaker has since learned. The accumulation builds a portrait of experience. This is a list poem structure.
action: Write 8 lines that each start with 'Before I knew...'
context: This is a list poem structure that builds a portrait of experience through accumulation. Each line reveals something learned — the repetition creates rhythm and the content creates depth.
constraint_note: Each line should reveal something the speaker has since learned. The accumulation should build a portrait of who they've become.
example: 'Before I knew that silence could be a choice. / Before I knew that some doors close quietly.' — Each line implies a story without telling it.

description: Write a scene set in heavy fog. 100 words. Fog removes the visual — you're forced to write with sound, touch, and spatial disorientation. What does a familiar place feel like when you can't see it?
action: Write a scene set in heavy fog. 100 words.
context: Fog removes the visual and forces you into sound, touch, and spatial disorientation. It's a natural constraint that produces atmospheric writing.
constraint_note: No clear visuals. Write with sound, touch, and the feeling of not being able to see. 100 words.
example: 'The road disappeared three meters ahead. A horn sounded from somewhere she couldn't place.' — Fog makes the familiar alien.

description: Write a 100-word scene where a character is eavesdropping. What do they hear? Do they wish they hadn't? Eavesdropping is a natural story engine — overheard information creates instant conflict.
action: Write a scene of a character eavesdropping. 100 words.
context: Overheard information creates instant conflict. Eavesdropping is a natural story engine — the character learns something they can't un-hear.
constraint_note: What do they hear? Do they wish they hadn't? 100 words.
example: 'She wasn't trying to listen. But the wall was thin and her name came through clearly.' — Accidental eavesdropping is more dramatic than intentional.

description: Write about the first time you remember being aware of money — having it, not having it, wanting something you couldn't afford. One specific scene. 100 words.
action: Write about the first time you remember being aware of money. One specific scene. 100 words.
context: Money memories are deeply personal and culturally specific. The first awareness of having or not having is a formative moment that most people have never written about. It's rich, untapped material.
constraint_note: One scene, not a summary. Having it, not having it, wanting something you couldn't afford. 100 words.
example: 'I was eight, standing outside a bookshop, counting coins in my palm. The book was forty rupees. I had thirty-two.' — Specific numbers make money memories real.

description: Write a scene where a character is trying to keep a secret during a conversation. 150 words. The reader knows the secret. The other character doesn't. This dramatic irony creates tension on every line.
action: Write a scene where a character hides a secret during a conversation. 150 words.
context: Dramatic irony — the reader knows what the character is hiding. Every innocent question from the other person becomes loaded. This creates tension on every line.
constraint_note: The reader knows the secret. The other character doesn't. 150 words.
example: 'How was your day?' becomes terrifying when the reader knows what the character did that afternoon.

description: Write 5 sentences about fire. Each sentence should use fire differently: as destruction, as warmth, as light, as memory, as metaphor. One element, five meanings.
action: Write 5 sentences about fire, each using it differently.
context: One element, five meanings — this trains you to see how a single image can carry completely different emotional weight depending on context. It's the foundation of symbolic writing.
constraint_note: Use fire as: destruction, warmth, light, memory, and metaphor. One sentence per meaning.
example: Destruction: 'The fire ate through the curtains in seconds.' Memory: 'I can still smell the bonfire from that November.' — Same word, different worlds.

description: Write a scene where a character is looking at themselves in a mirror. 100 words. The trap: don't use it to describe their appearance. Use it to show how they feel about themselves.
action: Write a scene of a character looking in a mirror. 100 words.
context: The mirror scene is a trap in fiction — writers use it to describe appearance. The real opportunity is to show what the character thinks about themselves.
constraint_note: Don't use it to describe their appearance. Use it to reveal their relationship with themselves. 100 words.
example: 'She looked at her reflection and looked away. Looked back. Adjusted nothing.' — The mirror reveals self-perception, not appearance.

description: Write a 100-word scene set at a chai stall. Capture the specific atmosphere: the hiss of the stove, the clatter of glass cups, the conversations overlapping. Make a reader who's never been to India smell the cardamom.
action: Write a scene at a chai stall. 100 words.
context: Chai stalls are sensory-rich, culturally specific settings. The hiss of the stove, the clatter of glass cups, the conversations — it's a world in a few square meters.
constraint_note: Capture the specific atmosphere: sounds, smells, the rhythm of the place. 100 words.
example: 'The stove hissed. He poured from a height, the tea arcing brown and steaming into the glass. Two rupees. No change.' — Specificity makes it real.

description: Write a scene where a character deletes a message before sending it. 80 words. What did the message say? Why didn't they send it? The unsent message is more interesting than the sent one.
action: Write a scene of a character deleting a message before sending it. 80 words.
context: The unsent message is a window into what someone wants to say but won't. The gap between the typed words and the silence that replaces them is pure character.
constraint_note: What did the message say? Why didn't they send it? 80 words.
example: 'She typed: I miss you. Stared at it. Deleted it letter by letter.' — Deleting letter by letter is different from hitting backspace once.

description: Write a 150-word scene where a parent and adult child have a conversation where both are being careful. Neither says what they really mean. The politeness is the conflict.
action: Write a scene between a parent and adult child where both are being careful. 150 words.
context: Politeness as conflict. When family members are too careful with each other, the courtesy itself becomes the problem — it signals distance where closeness should be.
constraint_note: Neither says what they really mean. The politeness IS the conflict. 150 words.
example: 'Would you like more rice?' / 'I'm okay, thanks.' / 'You're sure?' / 'I'm sure.' — Every exchange is a negotiation disguised as manners.

description: Write 10 nouns that are specific to your life — not 'car' but 'the dented Maruti with the broken AC.' Not 'house' but 'the second-floor flat with the blue gate.' Specificity is your signature.
action: Write 10 nouns that are specific to YOUR life.
context: Generic nouns ('car,' 'house') are invisible on the page. Specific nouns ('the dented Maruti with the broken AC') are alive. This stocks your personal inventory.
constraint_note: Not 'car' but 'the dented Maruti with the broken AC.' Not 'house' but 'the second-floor flat with the leaking balcony.' 10 nouns.
example: 'The steel tiffin with the dent on the lid. The Bata slippers that everyone in the house wore.' — Your nouns are your world.

description: Write a scene where a character is running late. 100 words. Capture the specific panic: the wrong keys, the missed auto, the phone at 2%. Urgency in writing comes from concrete obstacles, not abstract stress.
action: Write a scene of a character running late. 100 words.
context: Running late is controlled chaos — the wrong keys, the missed auto, the phone at 2%. The specific details of someone's lateness reveal their life.
constraint_note: Capture the specific panic. The details should be particular to this character's life. 100 words.
example: 'She grabbed the wrong keys, realized at the door, went back, couldn't find the right ones.' — Lateness is a cascade of small failures.

description: Write a 100-word scene where two characters are watching the same sunset but seeing completely different things. Same sky, two inner worlds. This is point of view in its purest form.
action: Write a scene of two characters watching the same sunset but seeing completely different things. 100 words.
context: Point of view in its purest form — same stimulus, different inner worlds. What a character notices reveals who they are.
constraint_note: Same sky, two perspectives. 100 words. The difference should reveal something about each character.
example: One sees beauty and feels peace. The other sees the day ending and feels dread. Same sunset — the difference is the character.

description: Write about a habit you have that no one else knows about. Not a secret — just something private. The small rituals people perform when alone are the most honest material for writing. 80 words.
action: Write about a private habit — something you do when no one's watching. 80 words.
context: The small rituals people perform when alone are the most honest material for writing. They reveal character without performance. This is what makes fictional characters feel real too.
constraint_note: Not a secret — just something private. A habit, a ritual, a small thing you do. 80 words.
example: 'I talk to the plants when I water them. Not words exactly — just a low sound, like I'm letting them know I'm here.' — Private habits are character in its purest form.

description: Write a scene where a character is teaching someone something. 150 words. The way a person teaches — patient or impatient, detailed or vague — reveals everything about them.
action: Write a scene of a character teaching someone something. 150 words.
context: How a person teaches — patient or impatient, detailed or vague — reveals their character more than what they teach. Teaching is a power dynamic.
constraint_note: The way they teach should reveal who they are. 150 words.
example: 'She said it once, clearly, then waited. She didn't repeat herself.' — Patient authority, shown through restraint.

description: Write a 100-word scene that takes place in the 30 seconds after a glass breaks. The sound, the freeze, the shards, the reaction. Slow a fast moment down. This is how you write action.
action: Write the 30 seconds after a glass breaks. 100 words.
context: The moment after a sudden event — the freeze, the shards, the silence — is more dramatic than the event itself. Slow motion in prose.
constraint_note: The sound, the freeze, the shards, the reaction. 100 words. Slow the moment down.
example: 'The sound was louder than it should have been. Nobody moved. A shard spun on the tile, then stopped.' — Stretch 30 seconds into a scene.

description: Write a scene where a character is choosing between two things at a shop — but the choice is really about something bigger in their life. 100 words. Small decisions as metaphors for large ones.
action: Write a scene of a character choosing between two things at a shop. 100 words.
context: Small decisions as metaphors for large ones. The character isn't really choosing between two shirts — they're choosing between two versions of themselves.
constraint_note: The choice is really about something bigger in their life. 100 words. Don't state the metaphor — let the reader feel it.
example: 'She held the red one in her left hand and the grey one in her right. The red one was nothing she'd normally wear.' — The choice is about more than color.

description: Write 5 closing lines for 5 different stories. A great last line should feel both surprising and inevitable. It should make the reader sit still for a moment after reading it.
action: Write 5 closing lines for 5 different stories.
context: A great last line should feel both surprising and inevitable. It should make the reader want to reread the story — or sit quietly for a moment.
constraint_note: 5 different stories, 5 last lines. Each should land with finality.
example: 'She never went back, and she never stopped wanting to.' — A great closing line contains the whole story in one sentence.

description: Write a scene where a character is walking home at night. 100 words. Not a thriller — just the ordinary experience of darkness, footsteps, shadows, and the relief of reaching your own door.
action: Write a scene of a character walking home at night. 100 words.
context: Night walking is ordinary but sensory-rich — darkness, footsteps, shadows, the feeling of being alone in a familiar place that looks different after dark.
constraint_note: Not a thriller. Just the ordinary experience of darkness, footsteps, and familiar streets made strange by night. 100 words.
example: 'The streetlight buzzed. His shadow stretched ahead of him, then disappeared, then stretched again.' — Night makes the familiar unfamiliar.

description: Write about something you're grateful for today. But write it as a scene, not a statement. Show the thing, the moment, the sensory detail. Gratitude in writing is showing, not telling. 80 words.
action: Write about something you're grateful for today — but as a scene, not a statement. 80 words.
context: Gratitude in writing is showing, not telling. 'I'm grateful for my morning coffee' is a statement. Describing the steam, the warmth, the quiet — that's writing that makes a reader feel gratitude too.
constraint_note: No statements of gratitude. Show the thing, the moment, the sensory detail. 80 words.
example: 'The balcony was warm at 7 AM. The chai was too hot to drink so I held it and watched the street wake up.' — The reader feels the gratitude without being told.

description: Write a 150-word scene where a character discovers that a friend has been talking about them behind their back. Don't write the confrontation — write the 5 minutes after they find out, alone.
action: Write the 5 minutes after a character discovers a friend has been talking about them behind their back. 150 words.
context: The aftermath is more dramatic than the confrontation. What someone does alone with a betrayal — before they decide how to respond — is raw, unperformed emotion.
constraint_note: Don't write the confrontation. Write the 5 minutes after they find out, alone. 150 words.
example: 'She put the phone down. Picked it up. Put it down again. Opened the fridge, closed it without taking anything.' — The body processes betrayal before the mind does.

description: Write a scene using only dialogue — no tags, no action, no description. Two characters, 10 lines each. The reader should know who's who from voice alone. If they can't, your characters sound the same.
action: Write a scene using only dialogue — no tags, no action, no description. Two characters, 10 lines each.
context: Pure dialogue is the ultimate test of voice distinction. If the reader can tell who's speaking without any help, your characters have real voices.
constraint_note: No 'he said,' no action beats, no description. Just words. The reader should know who's who.
example: 'You're late.' / 'I know.' / 'Again.' / 'I know.' — Even two words can carry a distinct voice.

description: Write a 100-word scene set at the exact moment day turns to night. That 10-minute window when the sky changes. Capture the transition — not day, not night, but the becoming.
action: Write a scene at the exact moment day turns to night. That 10-minute window. 100 words.
context: Twilight is a liminal space — between day and night, between certainty and uncertainty. The sky changes faster than you can describe it, which makes it perfect practice.
constraint_note: The 10-minute window when the sky changes. Capture the transition. 100 words.
example: 'The sky went from blue to orange to something that wasn't a color. The streetlights came on one by one, as if waking up.' — Twilight is a verb, not a noun.

description: Write a letter to yourself one year from now. Not goals or plans — tell future-you what today felt like. The weather, your mood, what you ate, what you're worried about. 100 words. This is time-capsule writing.
action: Write a letter to yourself one year from now. 100 words.
context: Time-capsule writing captures the texture of a specific day. Not goals or plans — the weather, your mood, what you ate, what you're worried about. Future-you will find this more valuable than any resolution.
constraint_note: Tell future-you what today felt like. Be specific and mundane — the details you'll forget are the ones worth recording.
example: 'It rained today and I forgot my umbrella. I'm eating too much sugar. The neighbour's dog barked all morning. I'm thinking about whether to call.' — Mundane specificity is the point.
